You have requested a clarification of one portion of our 
recent letter to Los Angeles City Councilman Hal Bernson 
(No. A-82-2l1). 
You are the Deputy Attorney General assigned to 
advise the State's participants on the Coliseum Commission. 
You 
have provided the following supplemental facts: 
The State's portion of tne triumvirate Coliseum Commission 
("Coliseum") is the 6th Agricultural District (The California 
Museum of Science and Industry). 
The Board of the 6th Agri-
cultural District is appointed by the Governor to fixed terms. 
This Board, in turn, selects from its own membersnip its 
representatives on the Coliseum. 
r have advised you that, based upon chese facts, the 
officials of tne 6th Agricultural District would be treated the 
same as city and county officials.* 
Thus, the distribution of 
free ticKets to the Raiders games, by the Coliseum (as described 
in the Bernson letter) to 6th Agricultural District officials 
would be considered part of their compensation and thus not a 
*Officials here include all high-ranking city and county 
officials, such as treasurer, city attorney, councy counsel, 
etc. 


Norman Flette

Page Two 
gift. 
Contrary to what tne Bernson letter stated, this is not 
limited to the 6th Agricultural District officials who actually 
sit on the Coliseum Commission. 
I trust that this-clarifies the matter to your satisfaction.

Advice, Our No. A-82-211 
Thank you for your recent letter requesting our advice 
regarding complimentary ticKets to Raiders football games 
at the Los Angeles Coliseum. 
We have previously received 
telephone inquiries from Anthony Saul Alperin of the City 
Attorney's Office regarding the issue and have rendered 
advice to him on the sUbject. 
However, it was unclear to 
him at that time who, in fact, was the provider of the 
tickets. 
You state the facts in your letter as follows: 
FACTS 
A question has been raised regarding the receipt 
of tickets from the Los Angeles Raiders football team 
by my office. 
I would appreciate your ruling on 
whether or not I now have a conflict of interest in 
matters dealing with the Los Angeles Me~orial Coliseum 
and the Los Angeles Raiders. 
On September 1, 1982, my office received four 
season tickets to the Raiders games which were 
scheduled to be played in the Los Angeles Memorial 
Coliseum. 
(The City of Los Angeles is a one-third 
partner with the County of Los Angeles and the State of 
California in the adminis tra tion of the Coliseum.) 
The 
first tickets were for a game played on August 29, 
1982, three days prior to receipt. 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983 
Page 2 
Tne ticKets for the gdmes to be played on 
December 18, and Decemoer 26, 1982, nave not been used 
and have been returned. 
The games scheduled for October 3, 10, 31 and 
November 7, 1982, were cancelled because of the NFL 
players' strike. 
Tne tickets for the games played on September 4, 
November 22, and December 5, 1982, were given to 
constituents and I have subsequently sent a check to 
pay fo r them. 
QUESTION 
My request is, "Does the conflict of interest 
occur upon receipt of the tickets (September 1), or for 
the use of ticKets, i.e., September 4, November 22, and 
December 5, wnich value is $216.00, under the $250 
limitation?" 
The balance of tickets were returned to 
the Raiders on December 15, 1982, with a check for 
$216. 
ANALYSIS 
Generally, the Commission's advice has been that if 
free tickets or passes are received by city officials to 
events held at a municipal stadium where the seats were 
reserved for the city's use as a part of the stadium lease 
agreement, then there is no gift made to the officials 
because the ticKets come from the city and are merely part 
of the "compensation" for the official. 
This advice was 
originally given in 1979 in response to a request from 
John W. Witt, City Attorney for San Diego. 
When Mr. Alperin contacted us in July, we were advised 
that the Los ill1geles Coliseum Commission (hereinafter the 
"Coliseum") operates the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum as a 
joint powers agency between the City of Los Angeles, the 
County of Los Angeles and the State of California (the 6th 
Agricultural District, which is the Museum of Science and 
Industry). 
As part of its lease agreements, the Coliseum 
routinely requires the lessee to set aside a certain number 
of tickets to the lessee's events which are then distri-
buted according to a list prepared by the Coliseum. 
The 
list consists of public officials who receive either two or 
four tickets to each event, depending on how many the 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983 
Page 3 
Coliseum decides to allocate to each specific official. 
The tickets are then provided and distributed by the lessee 
to this list of public officials. 
We were told to assume 
that the lessee has no veto power over the selections,ll 
out does have discretion over seat 10cation.~1 
We advised Mr. Alperin that, since the Coliseum is a 
triumvirate comprised of the City, County and State, the 
free tickets to City officials would be deemed to be part 
of the compensation which they received from the City. 
The 
same is true for County officials as well as for City and 
County staff actually assigned to the Coliseum. 
However, 
for state officials, only those who are actually members of 
the Coliseum, would fall into this category, along with 
state staff assigned to the Coliseum. 
As compensation from 
a public entity, the tickets do not represent reportable 
income and, hence, raise no conflict of interest problems. 
In response to our inquiries following receipt of your 
letter, we are now advised oy Mr. Alperin and his 
colleague, Tom Bonaventura, the deputy city attorney 
assigned to the Coliseum, of the following additional 
facts. 
1. 
The Coliseum adopted a policy in August 1~73 
relative to the issue of complimentary tickets to be 
distributed by lessees for Coliseum events. 
(See Exhibit 
"A".) 
General complimentary tickets to be distributed at 
the sole discretion of the lessee are limited to a maximum 
of 3,000 per event. 
This is to protect the Coliseum's 
interests since its revenues are based upon a 
II Mr. Alperin indicated that it was possible that 
the Coliseum merely prepares a "shopping list" of names of 
public officials, and the lessee may then select among 
those names in determining who will receive the free 
tickets (some 200+). 
Given that this was a speculative 
version of the facts, we advised Mr. Alperin on the basis 
of the assumption that the Coliseum actually selects the 
recipients of the ticKets. 
~I In point of fact, you have received "vpry good" 
seats according to the Coliseum. 
1 of the 226 seats 
provided by the Raiders are in good locations. 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983 
Page 4 
percentage of the gate receipts. 
In addition to the 
foregoing allotment, the policy provides for a maximum of 
340 additional complimentary tickets to be distributed by 
the lessee to local governmental officials. 
(See Exhioit 
"A".) 
A list is provided oy the Coliseum. 
The list 
consists of local pUblic officials, including Coliseum 
staff, and assorted political figures. 
Each official is 
alloted either four tickets or two tickets, according to 
the list. 
As new officials are elected or appointed to 
positions, the names on the list change. 
The list is 
periodically reviewed and revised by the Coliseum. 
Currently, tne revised list totals 318, although at one 
point it was down to 300. 
2. 
Botn of the limits for complimentary tickets 
contained in tne Coliseum policy are negotiable in the 
lease agreements. 
For instance, when the Rams were the 
lessee, one year they wanted to cut back on the number of 
complimentary tickets which they were required to hand out 
to public officials and politicians. 
In the preli~inary 
lease contract ("Agreement" - see Exhibit "B") entered into 
between the Coliseum and the Raiders for the 1982 season, 
the Raiders negotiated the number of required complimentary 
tickets down to 236. 
The Agreement calls for the Raiders 
to provide to the Coliseum 236 tickets for each Raiders 
horne game. 
The tickets shall be provided in such locations 
as the Raioers may determine; however, once the 150 suites 
are constructed by the Raiders, three suites shall be 
included in the allotment to the Coliseum. 
3. 
The agreement between the Raiders and the Coliseum" 
was reached in July 1982, and the pre-season games were 
scneduled to begin in August. 
Consequently, time frames 
were very short and ticket problems were prevalent. 
Ms. Margaret Farnum, Assistant Secretary to the Coliseum, 
had several conversations with Mr. Al LoCasale, a chief 
Raiders assistant, regarding the need for tne Raiders to 
make gooo on their contractual obligation to provide the 
complimentary tickets for the public officials in a timely 
fashion. 
Finally, Mr. LoCasale advised Ms. Farnum that the 
Raiders could not accomplish the task of distributing these 
tickets. 
He then delivered a box to Ms. Farnum's office 
containing 236 sets of season tickets to all of the 
scneduled horne games of the Raiders. 
The task of 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983 
Page 5 
distribution was turned over to her assistant, who pared 
down the list of 318 to 236 and distributed the tickets to 
that selected group,l/ first removing one ticKet for an 
exhibition game which had already Deen played. 
You were 
one of the recipients of these tickets. 
Ms. Farnum's 
assistant, with her input, determined the location of the 
tickets distributed to the various recipients. 
ANALYSIS 
You received the tickets on Septmeber 1, 1982. 
The 
value of them is the fair marKet value (see our HopKins 
~pinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 107, No. 77-022, December 8, 1977, 
copy enclosed). 
In the case of these Raiders tickets, that 
is $18 eacn. 
However, since the August 29, 1982, game was 
played prior to receipt of the tickets by you, the value of 
tickets to that game will not be counted in the total. 
At 
the time you received the ticKets, there were nine home 
games remaining on the Raiders sChedule. 
Thus the value of 
the ticKets received was 9 x 4 x $18, which equals $648. 
However, because of the intervening strike, that value was 
reduced to $360. 
Therefore, if the ticKets were a 
1/ 
Some of the recipients returned the tickets, 
unused, within 30 days of receipt. 
Ms. Farnum kept these 
tickets for others on the list who hadn't received any. 
Unclaimed tiCkets were distributed to Coliseum staff, none 
were returned to the Raiders. 
Some recipients paid for all 
or a portion of the tickets within 30 days of receipt. 
Checks were made payable to the Raiders, but were sent to 
Ms. Farnum, whO then forwarded them to tne Raiders. 
She 
had advised at least some of the recipients that the checKs 
should be made payable to the Raiders, because she assumed 
that tne Raiders were the donors. 
Written communications 
were sent by the Coliseum to its members and staff and 
sUbsequently to all recipients. 
These are attached as 
Exhibit "C". 
The advice and arithmetic contained therein 
is not necessarily accurate. 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983 
Page 6 
giftil from the Raiders, both the disclosure~1 and 
disqualificationil provisions of the Act would be 
applicable. 
Under the circumstances, we do not think that the 
Raiders were a donor of the ticKets. 
We limit our 
il Government Code Section 82028, which defines the 
term "gift" for purposes of the Political Reform Act, 
provides, in part, that: 
"Gift" means, except as provided in sUbdivision 
(b), any payment to the extent that consideration of 
equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value 
unless the reoate or discount is made in the regular 
course of business to memoers of the puolic without 
regard to official status . ... 
Since you did not return the ticKets within 30 days, tne 
exception in Section 82028 (see below) does not apply. 
(b) 
The term "gift" does not include: 
* * * 
(2)Gifts wnich are not used and which, within 
30 days after receipt, are returned to the donor 
or delivered to a charitable organization without 
being claimed as a charitable contribution for tax 
purpos e si 
~ Receipt of gifts aggregating $50 or more in value 
from anyone source during the reporting period must be 
disclosed on an official's Statement of Economic 
Interests. 
Government Code Section 87207(a) (1). 
il A gift is income under the Political Reform Act. 
Section 82030(a). 
A source of income of $250 or more 
constitutes a financial interest. 
Sections 87100, 
87103 (c). 
A conflict of interest requiring disqualifi-
cation could only arise as to those governmental decisions 
which would foreseeably have a material financial effect 
upon the Raiders. 
Other decisions could have been made 
you even if the Raiders had been a source of income to yo . 


Ha 1 Bernson 
January 19, 1983 
Page 7 
conclusion to the precise facts of this situation. 
The 
Raiders were required Dy tne lease agreement to supply the 
236 tickets. 
However, the Coliseum exercised all the 
discretionary control over the distribution of these 
tickets. 
The Coliseum decided which tickets, and how many 
of them, went to each individual official. 
Consequently, 
as per our previous advice to Mr. Alperin, the Coliseum is 
to be considered the source of the tickets in this 
situation. 
The Raiders merely fulfilled a contractual 
ooligation wnich was placed upon them by tne Coliseum as 
part of the overall contract. 
Had the Ra~ders wished to 
donate complimentary tickets to various officials, they 
clearly nad a sufficient allotment of their own from which 
to do so. 
In the future, if discretion as to the recipients of 
these complimentary tickets is vested in the Raiders (or 
any otller lessee), then they are the donor and the tiCkets 
are a gift. 
In that situation, receipt of $250 or more in 
tiCkets which are neither returned nor paid for within 30 
days would create t11e basis for a conflict of interest 7/ 
on tne part of a puolic official who receives such a gift. 
I trust that this letter has satisfactorily responded to 
your question. 
Should you have any questions regarding this 
advice, please contact me at 916/322-5901.

ift and for how much the check

Generally, the Commission's advice has been that if 
free tickets or passes are received by city officials to 
events held at a municipal stadium where the seats were 
reserved for the city's use as a part of the stadium lease 
agreement, then there is no gift made to the officials 
because the ticKets come from the city and are merely part 
of the "compensation" for the official. 
This advice was 
originally given in 1979 in response to a request from 
John W. Witt, City Attorney for San Diego. 
When Mr. Alperin contacted us in July, we were advised 
that the Los ill1geles Coliseum Commission (hereinafter the 
"Coliseum") operates the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum as a 
joint powers agency between the City of Los Angeles, the 
County of Los Angeles and the State of California (the 6th 
Agricultural District, which is the Museum of Science and 
Industry). 
As part of its lease agreements, the Coliseum 
routinely requires the lessee to set aside a certain number 
of tickets to the lessee's events which are then distri-
buted according to a list prepared by the Coliseum. 
The 
list consists of public officials who receive either two or 
four tickets to each event, depending on how many the 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983  
Coliseum decides to allocate to each specific official. 
The tickets are then provided and distributed by the lessee 
to this list of public officials. 
We were told to assume 
that the lessee has no veto power over the selections,ll 
out does have discretion over seat 10cation.~1 
We advised Mr. Alperin that, since the Coliseum is a 
triumvirate comprised of the City, County and State, the 
free tickets to City officials would be deemed to be part 
of the compensation which they received from the City. 
The 
same is true for County officials as well as for City and 
County staff actually assigned to the Coliseum. 
However, 
for state officials, only those who are actually members of 
the Coliseum, would fall into this category, along with 
state staff assigned to the Coliseum. 
As compensation from 
a public entity, the tickets do not represent reportable 
income and, hence, raise no conflict of interest problems. 
In response to our inquiries following receipt of your 
letter, we are now advised oy Mr. Alperin and his 
colleague, Tom Bonaventura, the deputy city attorney

facts.

General complimentary tickets to be distributed at 
the sole discretion of the lessee are limited to a maximum 
of 3,000 per event.

interests since its revenues are based upon a
